aliases: Sweet and Spicy Boneless Fried Chicken

english: Sweet and Spicy Boneless Fried Chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean boneless chilli fried chicken; closest match is generic fried chicken food product.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers coated fried chicken; no specific code for Korean-style chilli sauce variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical sharing portion of boneless chilli chicken is approximately 500 g, suitable for 2–3 people as part of a shared Korean fried chicken meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried (SR Legacy ID 05062) combined with estimated sauce contribution from USDA Branded and HPB Singapore nutrient data for sweet chilli sauce
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Korean boneless chilli fried chicken. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (meat only) as the protein/fat base, with carbohydrate and sodium adjusted upward to reflect the wheat-flour/cornstarch coating and sweet chilli sauce glaze. The input record's per-100g values (energy 308 kcal, protein 20 g, fat 20.8 g, carbohydrate 10.8 g, sodium 533 mg) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained as the working values. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on battered/coated fried chicken with sweet sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean boneless chilli fried chicken. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the analogous food category of battered/coated fried chicken with sweet sauce coating from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables. The wheat-flour/cornstarch coating and sweet chilli glaze contribute moderate-GI refined carbohydrates. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (65 × 54 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 35.1, classified as high GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 533 mg per 100 g — well above the 400 mg/100 g threshold — driven by soy sauce, sweet chilli sauce, and added salt in the marinade and glaze.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100–150 g and request reduced soy sauce and sweet chilli sauce; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 2,665 mg sodium, exceeding the daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg in a single dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate coating (wheat flour and cornstarch) combined with sweet chilli sauce sugar contributes a medium GI of 65 and a high glycemic load of 35 per 500 g serving, with only 0.67 g/100 g dietary fibre to blunt the glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, restrict to a 100 g portion, pair with non-starchy vegetables, and request reduced sweet chilli sauce or a sugar-free chilli alternative to lower the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 20.8 g/100 g with saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.08 g/100 g from deep-frying; cholesterol 75 mg/100 g; high sodium 533 mg/100 g compounds cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish due to the combined burden of saturated fat, trans fat, high sodium, and cholesterol; if consumed occasionally, limit to a 100 g portion and request grilled or baked preparation to eliminate deep-frying-derived trans fats and reduce saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g is at the upper threshold; chilli in gochujang and sweet chilli sauce may aggravate nightshade-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its deep-fried form; request grilled or baked preparation and reduced chilli sauce to lower AGE burden and nightshade exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boneless Chilli Chicken is a Korean-style fried chicken dish consisting of crispy, boneless chicken pieces coated in a seasoned wheat-flour and cornstarch batter, deep-fried until golden and crunchy, then glazed with a sweet and spicy chilli sauce typically made from gochujang, sweet chilli sauce, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil. The dish is popular in Singapore as a Korean fried chicken restaurant offering, often served as a large sharing plate alongside beer. The texture is crispy on the outside with juicy, tender chicken inside, and the flavour profile is a balance of sweet, spicy, savoury, and umami notes. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 308 kcal, 20 g protein, 20.8 g fat (5 g saturated), 10.8 g carbohydrate, and 533 mg sodium. The high sodium content makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, and the refined carbohydrate coating with sweet sauce creates a medium glycaemic index (estimated 65) and high glycaemic load per serving, making it a high-concern food for type 2 diabetes management. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products relevant to cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. Allergens include gluten (wheat flour), soy, and sesame. In Singapore, this dish is commonly known in Malay as Ayam Cili Tanpa Tulang and is widely available through Korean fried chicken chains and food delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Korean chilli fried chicken?
Is boneless chilli chicken suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of Korean fried chicken?
Is Korean boneless chilli chicken halal?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Korean boneless chilli fried chicken exists in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (meat only) with coating and sauce adjustments consistent with the input record's values. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for Korean-style boneless chilli fried chicken are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024 for this specific preparation. The input record's per_100g values were sourced from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and are treated as estimated values; they have been cross-checked for plausibility against USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data and retained as working values. Two portions were listed in the input with is_default: true for both; the 500 g sharing portion is used as the default serving for GL calculation. The dish is Korean-style in origin but is sold in Singapore; multilingual naming therefore satisfies both the Korean cuisine requirement (Korean native script) and the Singaporean serving-market requirement (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Malay local name: Ayam Cili Tanpa Tulang. Tamil local name: எலும்பில்லா மிளகாய் கோழி. Hindi local name: बोनलेस चिली चिकन. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed from input JSON with USDA SR Legacy nutrient adaptation and GI analogous estimate.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; clarified multilingual justification.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory Malay (Ayam Cili Tanpa Tulang), Tamil (எலும்பில்லா மிளகாய் கோழி), and Hindi (बोनलेस चिली चिकन) local names and aliases to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 validator requirements for the Singaporean serving market.
